nativeRange.habitat: Swampy lowland wetlands, marshes, irrigation ditches, rice paddies, and still to slow-moving tropical and subtropical freshwater. Source-attested origin is Asian; some references treat the taxon as effectively pan-tropical/cosmopolitan due to its synonymy with C. thalictroides / C. cornuta.

decorPreferences: fine gravel or planted-tank substrate when planted
soft rooting — bury only the base of the rhizome

summary: An Asian-origin aquatic fern marketed as the 'Lace Leaf' form of Water Sprite, with notably finer and more dissected foliage than the standard Ceratopteris thalictroides. Fast-growing, beginner-friendly, and equally happy planted in the background or left floating at the surface.
careNotes: The 'Lace Leaf' Water Sprite is a finer-leaved Ceratopteris form distinguished from the common Water Sprite (C. thalictroides) by its more deeply dissected, lacier fronds. Its specific status is in flux: some authorities recognise Ceratopteris siliquosa as a distinct Asian species, while others synonymise it with C. cornuta or treat it as a form of C. thalictroides. In the hobby it is consistently sold as a separate offering for its finer foliage. Care is essentially the same as standard Water Sprite. It is generally considered beginner-friendly and can be grown two ways: planted in fine gravel, sand, or aquasoil as a midground-to-background specimen, or left floating at the surface where it produces a feathery canopy with trailing roots that fry and shrimp use for cover. Lighting is best in the moderate-to-bright range; stronger light and water-column nutrients drive faster, bushier growth. CO2 is not required (sources are split between 'not required' and 'recommended for optimal lacy growth'); supplementation accelerates an already-fast growth rate. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral, with reported tolerances roughly 22–28 °C and pH 6.0–7.5 across the sources consulted. The plant is a heavy water-column feeder and strips nitrate/phosphate quickly, making it useful for stabilising new tanks and breeding setups. Rooting is weak relative to true substrate plants — bury only the base of the rhizome (never the crown) when planting, or simply float it. Older leaves produce adventitious plantlets along their margins that detach and root on their own; this is the practical propagation route in aquaria. Taxonomy/sourcing caveat: one consulted vendor (Bucephalandra/Buce Plant) listed the family as Ceratophyllaceae, which is incorrect (that family is hornworts/Ceratophyllum); Ceratopteris belongs to Pteridaceae (Polypodiales), inherited here from the well-established placement of C. thalictroides.
breedingNotes: Propagated vegetatively by adventitious plantlets that form on the margins of mature leaves; the plantlets develop their own roots while still attached and detach (or are gently snipped off) to be replanted or left to float. Lateral shoots from the base can also be separated. Spore reproduction occurs only in emersed/fertile fronds and is impractical in submerged aquarium culture.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source for the 'Lace Leaf' trade form: lighting (moderate, faster/bushier with stronger light), CO2 (not required), growth rate (fast under good conditions), temperature 71–82 °F (~22–28 °C), placement (background planted or floating), substrate (fine gravel/planted-tank substrate; not required if floating), fertilisation (benefits from nutrient-rich environment, draws nutrients from water column), and care level (easy). Native range, family, and detailed pH/hardness/size not specified by this source.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked: common names (Water Sprite, Lace Leaf, Indian Fern), origin described as 'cosmopolitan', lighting (low–medium), CO2 (recommended), growth rate (moderate-to-fast), placement (stem plant, floated or planted), adult height 6–15 in (~15–38 cm), pH 6.5–7.5, substrate not critical (weak rooting), foliar nutrient absorption, propagation by cutting along the stem, care level (easy). Note: this source lists the family as 'Ceratophyllaceae', which is incorrect (Ceratophyllaceae is the hornwort family) — Ceratopteris is in Pteridaceae; this entry uses the correct Pteridaceae placement inherited from the well-established C. thalictroides taxonomy.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ceratopteris (genus)
additional.notes: Background reference on the genus Ceratopteris (family Pteridaceae, order Polypodiales) and the ongoing taxonomic revision of the C. thalictroides / C. cornuta / C. richardii complex. The article does not list C. siliquosa as a currently-accepted species name, supporting the documented synonymy/flux: aquarium trade name 'Lace Leaf' applied to Ceratopteris siliquosa is variably treated as a synonym of C. cornuta or as a form of C. thalictroides depending on authority.
